steps.title: Identify the author's challenge to the energy ladder
steps.content: The passage presents the energy ladder as a common explanation for fuel choice shifts, then immediately uses however and calls the model reductive. This signals the author is questioning whether the model adequately explains fuel choice decisions.

steps.title: Match the challenge to the choices
steps.content: Choice A—to challenge a common explanation—is the direct match for what calling something reductive accomplishes. The other three either misidentify the target (fuel prices, not choice), reverse causality, or mistake the purpose (comparing countries rather than evaluating a model's adequacy). That matches choice A — A is correct.
